tier_intro: Who are your customers, really? The more your AI knows about who it's talking to, the better it handles every conversation — from how it explains things to how it responds when someone pushes back.

tier_complete.headline: Customer profile built.
tier_complete.body: Your AI agent now knows who it's serving — their language, their pain points, their objections, and what makes them loyal. It will stop talking like a bot and start talking like someone who understands your customers. Next: set the communication rules.

questions.question: Describe your ideal customer. Who do you most love to work with?
questions.placeholder: e.g. 'Small business owners who are growing fast but don't have an IT team', 'Parents of kids aged 5-12 who care about screen time', 'CFOs at logistics companies with 50-200 employees'
questions.meta_profiling: Specificity of ICP reveals market focus. Vague = still finding product-market fit. Very specific = mature positioning. Note B2B vs. B2C and whether they focus on the buyer or the user.
questions.why_this_matters: AI adapts its language, depth, and approach to whoever it's talking to. Knowing the ideal customer shapes every interaction — tone, vocabulary, patience level, assumed knowledge.

questions.question: Do you serve distinctly different types of customers?
questions.meta_profiling: Multiple segment selection = AI needs segment detection logic. Single segment = simpler, more focused AI behavior.
questions.why_this_matters: When AI knows a customer is enterprise vs. consumer, it changes how it handles pricing questions, compliance topics, and escalation paths automatically.

questions.question: Why do customers come to you? What problem are they trying to solve?

options.label: Save time — they're too busy doing it themselves
Save money — looking for a better deal
Fix a specific problem — something broke or isn't working
Replace a competitor or outdated solution
Access expertise they don't have in-house
Meet a regulatory or compliance requirement
Reduce risk or avoid a mistake
Pure convenience — you're the easiest option
Trust — they were referred by someone they know

questions.meta_profiling: Dominant pain point shapes how AI frames your value proposition. 'Save time' = efficiency language. 'Fix problem' = urgency language. 'Trust' = relationship language.
questions.why_this_matters: AI proactively speaks to customer pain when relevant — 'I know this can be frustrating when...' hits differently when AI actually knows the context.

questions.question: What are the top 5 questions customers ask you most often?
questions.placeholder: List them one per line. These are the questions your AI will answer best.
questions.meta_profiling: Actual FAQ content reveals true customer priorities vs. assumed ones. Questions about price = undifferentiated. Questions about fit = still evaluating. Questions about how it works = already sold, needs confidence.
questions.why_this_matters: Top 5 questions drive 80% of customer conversations. AI handles these first — perfectly and instantly. If you're uploading an FAQ doc in B5, this is a preview.

questions.question: When someone hesitates to buy or sign up, it's usually because...

options.label: Fit — 'Not sure this is right for me'
Process — 'I need to check with my team / boss'
Timing — 'Not the right time right now'
Trust — 'I don't know you well enough yet'
Complexity — 'Seems complicated to set up or switch'
Status quo — 'I already have something that works'
No urgency — 'I'll come back to this later'

questions.meta_profiling: Objection types map directly to sales maturity. 'Price' objections in premium brands = positioning gap. 'Trust' objections = brand awareness issue. 'Complexity' = UX problem.
questions.why_this_matters: AI preemptively addresses objections before customers raise them — and handles them gracefully when they do. This is the difference between a chatbot that converts and one that doesn't.

questions.question: How do you typically respond to your #1 objection? What do you say?
questions.placeholder: Give AI your actual best response — the one your best salesperson uses
questions.meta_profiling: Quality of objection response reveals sales sophistication. A good response = AI replicates it at scale. No response = opportunity to build one together.
questions.why_this_matters: AI delivers your best salesperson's response to every customer — not the average one, the best one. This alone can improve conversion rate.

questions.question: In a B2B context: who actually makes the decision to buy?

options.label: The person we're talking to makes the decision
They need their manager's or boss's sign-off
A team or committee decides together
Procurement or finance team controls the budget

questions.meta_profiling: Decision-maker distance affects how AI handles urgency and buy signals. Procurement = formal, paperwork, compliance. Committee = needs consensus-building content.
questions.why_this_matters: AI talks differently to the decision-maker than to the influencer. Knowing this prevents pushing for a close with someone who literally can't say yes.

questions.question: What's a typical customer's budget range when they come to you?

options.label: Varies widely — depends on the project

questions.meta_profiling: Budget range calibrates how AI discusses pricing, frames value, and handles price objections. High-value sales need more careful handling.
questions.why_this_matters: AI calibrates its language around budget context. A €50k sale requires trust-building and patience. A €20 sale needs friction-free speed.

questions.question: How long does it typically take from first contact to a purchase or signed contract?

options.label: Instant — people buy on the spot
Same day — usually resolved in one conversation
A few days — short consideration period

questions.meta_profiling: Sales cycle length determines how much AI should push for immediate action vs. nurture over time. Long cycles = AI should offer to follow up, provide resources.
questions.why_this_matters: AI shouldn't push for a close if the customer is 2 weeks into a 3-month sales cycle. Knowing the cycle prevents pushy, tone-deaf interactions.

questions.question: What words and phrases do YOUR customers actually use? Paste some examples.
questions.placeholder: e.g. Real phrases from emails, support tickets, reviews, or calls. 'we need a solution that...', 'the problem is always...', 'can you guys...'
questions.meta_profiling: Customer vocabulary is gold. It reveals the gap between how the company talks and how customers actually think. AI bridges this gap.
questions.why_this_matters: Customers feel understood when AI uses THEIR language, not company jargon. This is the fastest way to make AI feel human to your customers.

questions.question: What makes customers LOVE you? What do they say in positive reviews or referrals?

options.label: That you actually solve the problem
Easy to deal with, no hassle
Quality of the actual product / work

questions.meta_profiling: Satisfaction drivers should align with USP. If they don't — gap between what company thinks its USP is and what customers actually value.
questions.why_this_matters: AI proactively reinforces what makes you great — especially when customers are evaluating you. It mentions what you're genuinely known for at the right moment.

questions.question: When customers leave or don't come back, what's usually the reason?

options.label: Life or business change on their end

questions.meta_profiling: Churn reasons reveal the company's biggest retention vulnerabilities. 'Slow response' = AI intervention opportunity. 'Found cheaper' = value perception problem.
questions.why_this_matters: AI actively works to prevent the most common churn triggers — faster responses, proactive issue resolution, reminding customers of value before they drift.

questions.question: How do new customers typically find you?
questions.meta_profiling: Acquisition channels reveal how much the customer knows about you at first contact. Word-of-mouth = already warm, needs confirmation. Google search = cold, needs to be convinced.
questions.why_this_matters: AI adapts based on how warm the conversation is. A referred customer needs different handling than someone who just searched Google and clicked the first result.

questions.question: How knowledgeable are your typical customers about your industry or product category?

options.label: Novice — they're new to this, need things explained from basics
Some knowledge — they know the basics, not the details
Intermediate — they know the industry, can have a real conversation
Expert — they know more than most, prefer peer-level communication
Mixed — we get both beginners and experts

questions.meta_profiling: Sophistication level shapes vocabulary complexity, how much AI explains vs. assumes, and whether to use technical shorthand or plain language.
questions.why_this_matters: Talking down to an expert is insulting. Over-assuming with a novice is confusing. This calibrates the default communication level for every interaction.
